verses:
śrī⋅nārada uvāca⋅
navīna⋅nīrada⋅śyāmaṁ nīlendīvara⋅locanam
vallavī⋅nandanaṁ vande kṛṣṇaṁ gopāla⋅rūpiṇam

sphurad⋅varhi⋅dalodvaddha⋅nīla⋅kuñcita⋅mūrdhajam
kadamba⋅kusumodvaddha⋅vanamālā⋅vibhūṣitam

gaṇḍa⋅maṇḍalasaṁsargi⋅calat⋅kuñcita⋅kuntalam
sthūlaṁ muktāphalodāra⋅hārodyotita⋅vakṣasam

hemāṅga⋅tulākoṭi⋅kirīṭojjvala⋅vigraham
manda⋅mārūta⋅saṅkṣobha⋅calitāmvara⋅sañcayam

rūcirauṣṭha⋅puṭa⋅nyasta⋅vaṁśī⋅madhura⋅niḥsvanaiḥ
lasad⋅gopālikā⋅ceto mohayantaṁ punaḥ punaḥ

vallavī⋅vadanāmbhoja ⋅ madhu⋅pāna⋅madhu⋅vratam
kṣobhayantaṁ manas tāsāṁ sasmerāpāṅga⋅vīkṣaṇaiḥ

jauvanod bhinna dehābhiḥ saṁsaktābhiḥ parasparam
vicitrāmvara⋅bhūṣābhir gopa⋅nārībhir āvṛtam

prabhinnāñjana⋅kālindī⋅jala⋅keli⋅kalotsukam
jodhayantaṁ kvacid gopān vyāharantaṁ gavāṁ gaṇam

kālindī⋅jala⋅saṁsargi⋅śītalānila⋅sevite
kadamba⋅pādapacchāye sthitaṁ vṛndāvane kvacit

ratna⋅bhūdhara⋅saŋlagna⋅ratnāsana⋅parigraham
kalpa⋅pādapa⋅madhyastha⋅hema⋅maṇḍapikā⋅gatam

vasanta⋅kusumāmoda⋅surabhī⋅kṛta⋅diṅ⋅mukhe
govardhana⋅girau ramye sthitaṁ rāsa⋅rasotsukam

savya⋅hasta⋅tala⋅nyasta⋅giri⋅varyāta⋅patrakam
khaṇḍitākhaṇḍalon⋅mukta⋅muktāsāra⋅ghanāghanam

veṇu⋅vādya⋅mahollāsa⋅kṛta⋅huṅkāra⋅niḥsvanaiḥ
sa⋅vatsair unmukhaiḥ śaśvad gokulair abhivīkṣitam

kṛṣṇam eva tu gāyadbhis tac⋅ceṣṭā⋅vaśa⋅vartibhiḥ
daṇḍa⋅pāśodyata⋅karair gopālai rūpa⋅śobhitamnāradādyair muni⋅śreṣṭhair veda⋅vedāṅga⋅pāragaiḥ
prīti⋅susnigdhayā vācā stūyamānaṁ parāt param

ja evaṁ cintayed devaṁ bhaktyā saṁstauti mānavaḥ
trisandhyaṁ tasya tuṣṭo’sau dadāti varam īpsitam

rāja⋅vallabhatām eti bhavet sarva⋅jana⋅priyaḥ
acalāṁ śriyam āpnoti sa vāgmī jāyate dhruvam

iti śrī⋅nārada⋅pañcarātre
jñānāmṛta⋅sāre caturtha⋅rātre gopāla⋅stotraṁ nāma
ṣaṣṭho’dhyāyaḥ
en_translation:
Śrī Nārada said: I offer my respectful obeisances to the son of the cowherd woman, Kṛṣṇa, whose form is that of a cowherd boy. His complexion is dark like a fresh raincloud, and His eyes resemble blue lotus flowers.

His dark, curly hair is tied with a shining peacock feather, and He is adorned with a garland of forest flowers strung with Kadamba blossoms.

His moving, curly locks of hair touch His round cheeks, and His broad chest is illuminated by a necklace of large pearls.

His form shines with golden armlets, waistbands, and a crown. His garments flutter in the agitation of the gentle breeze.

With the sweet sounds of the flute placed upon His beautiful lips, He again and again enchants the minds of the shining cowherd girls.

He is the bee drinking the honey of the lotus faces of the cowherd women. He agitates their minds with His smiling sidelong glances.

He is surrounded by the wives of the cowherds, who are decorated with wonderful clothes and ornaments, whose bodies are blossoming with youth, and who are attached to one another.

He is eager for the artful sports in the waters of the Yamunā, which are dark like crushed collyrium. Sometimes He fights with the cowherd boys and sometimes He calls out to the herd of cows.

Sometimes He stays in Vṛndāvana, in the shade of a Kadamba tree, served by the cooling breeze touching the waters of the Yamunā.

He accepts a jeweled throne located on a jeweled mountain. He has entered a golden pavilion situated in the midst of desire trees.

He stays on the beautiful Govardhana Hill, where the directions are perfumed by the fragrance of spring flowers, eager for the mellows of the Rāsa dance.

He holds the best of mountains like an umbrella in the palm of His left hand, shielding Vraja from the dense clouds releasing torrents of rain sent by the angry Indra.

He is constantly gazed upon by the herds of cows with their calves, who look up with faces lifted, making loud lowing sounds in great jubilation at the music of His flute.

He is beautified by the cowherd boys, who sing only of Kṛṣṇa, who are under the control of His activities, and who hold sticks and ropes in their uplifted hands. He is the Supreme beyond the Supreme, praised with words full of affection by the best of sages led by Nārada, who are learned in the Vedas and Vedāṅgas.

A human being who thinks of that Lord in this way and praises Him with devotion three times a day pleases Him, and He grants the desired boon.

He becomes dear to the king and beloved by all people. He attains steady opulence and certainly becomes an orator.

Thus ends the Gopāla-stotra in the sixth chapter of the fourth night of the Jñānāmṛta-sāra of the Śrī Nārada-pañcarātra.
